caveats: a: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was found.
a: The strongest grading results use a lab-collected dataset and do not establish field labor savings.
a: The supplied compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis.
rho: Patent and research evidence show feasibility, not broad commercial adoption.
rho: Census AI data cover all US businesses rather than planing mills.
rho: The 2024 recordable injury rate of 5.4 per 100 workers raises the burden of safe integration.
e: Industry classification does not identify who owns the input lumber or whether processing is contracted as a service.
e: Made-to-order product sales can resemble toll processing but may not meet the fixed lens.
e: The supplied firm count is a margin-bridged estimate based on 2022 receipts and a January 2026 sector margin.
s5: Plans may not close.
s5: The survey population is broader than industrial LMM firms.
s5: Its ownership-transfer category contains events excluded by the screen.
q: The AP evidence concerns construction-material pass-through, not retention of AI savings by planing firms.
q: No industry study directly measures five-year productivity-benefit retention.
q: Capture differs between toll conversion and differentiated cut-stock programs.
d5: CBO projections were published before subsequent policy and immigration changes.
d5: Softwood-lumber demand is broader than the current service basket.
d5: The remodeling consumption figures are from 2014 and show historical volume, not a forecast.
o: Insourcing is difficult to separate from supplier substitution in available evidence.
o: Operator need is higher for variable, graded stock than for standardized planing.
o: No direct survey measures customers' future self-service plans.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Eligible firms earn repeat B2B revenue by converting purchased or customer-specified lumber into surfaced, resized, graded, or cut stock; pricing may be per board foot, per unit, per processing step, or embedded in a processed-product price.
lens.excluded_activities: Commodity lumber or standard cut-stock sales without a recurring processing relationship, sawmills processing logs, captive internal remanufacturing, brokers and distributors without processing, software vendors, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms repeatedly providing outsourced resawing, surfacing, planing, sizing, grading, cut-stock, or related lumber-processing services to external industrial, distribution, and construction customers.

a.bridge: The occupation mix is observed for broader other-wood-product manufacturing and O*NET production-helper tasks, while research and a patent demonstrate AI grading and line-flow capabilities for lumber and planer mills. These are mapped to wage-weighted NAICS 321912 work. The bridge may overstate substitution because research accuracy is laboratory-based and robotics are separate capital equipment, but may understate high-value optimization of grading and recovery.
caveats: No six-digit occupation-by-task dataset was found.
The strongest grading results use a lab-collected dataset and do not establish field labor savings.
The supplied compensation ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to an IPS basis.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted time-and-motion study across 10-15 NAICS 321912 plants, separating grading, inspection, line watching, handling, setup, maintenance diagnosis, scheduling, order entry, and customer documentation, with current scanner and optimizer penetration.
a.rationale: The work is dominated by saw and planer operation, feeding, off-bearing, material movement, inspection, grading, packaging, maintenance, and scheduling. Physical handling limits AI-only substitution. AI vision can detect knots, cracks, holes, wane, dimensional defects, board-position problems, and line-flow anomalies; administrative work and optimization are also exposed. The resulting exposed share is meaningful but well below half of labor.

d5.bridge: CBO housing starts and Forest Service softwood-lumber demand and remodeling data are translated to demand for recurring resawing and planing services. They cover broader lumber end markets and do not isolate outsourced processing; CBO's forecast may overstate current demand if mortgage or immigration assumptions weaken, while remodeling and industrial uses provide diversification.
caveats: CBO projections were published before subsequent policy and immigration changes.
Softwood-lumber demand is broader than the current service basket.
The remodeling consumption figures are from 2014 and show historical volume, not a forecast.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year US board-foot forecasts by species and end use from Forest Economic Advisors, Random Lengths/Fastmarkets, WWPA, and the Forest Service, paired with capacity, import, remanufacturing, and outsourcing-share data specific to resawing and planing.
d5.rationale: CBO projected 1.68 million average annual housing starts in 2025-2029 versus 1.30 million over the prior decade, while the Forest Service estimates housing-start elasticity of 0.59 for softwood lumber. Repair and remodeling was also the first- or second-largest market for several wood products and consumed 13.5 billion board feet of softwood lumber in 2014. These sources support modest real five-year growth, with cyclical, tariff, efficiency, and substitution risks.

caveats: Industry classification does not identify who owns the input lumber or whether processing is contracted as a service.
Made-to-order product sales can resemble toll processing but may not meet the fixed lens.
The supplied firm count is a margin-bridged estimate based on 2022 receipts and a January 2026 sector margin.
e.change_evidence: Classify the supplied 204-firm LMM estimate by revenue from toll processing, customer-owned material, specification-driven repeat programs, made-to-order cut stock, standard inventory products, and pure distribution, using websites, customer contracts, and management interviews.
e.rationale: Unlike many manufacturing codes, resawing and planing purchased lumber can be a recurring outsourced processing service, especially when performed to customer dimensions, grades, moisture targets, or schedules. Standard cut stock manufactured for inventory and commodity resale remains product revenue. The mix is not published, so a substantial but nonmajority eligible share is estimated.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 321912 ($693,918,023) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 321912 ($8,327,123,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $385,105 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 321912); class <5: 186 firms, avg revenue/firm $736,081 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $107,836 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 109 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,804,963 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $264,427 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 108 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,197,111 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $468,377 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 172 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,879,901 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,740,406 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 32 firms, avg revenue/firm $41,343,562 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,056,832 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 36 firms, avg revenue/firm $118,940,167 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $17,424,734 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 204

caveats: Insourcing is difficult to separate from supplier substitution in available evidence.
Operator need is higher for variable, graded stock than for standardized planing.
No direct survey measures customers' future self-service plans.
o.change_evidence: Interviews with lumber distributors, component makers, millwork firms, builders, and furniture manufacturers on five-year plans to insource processing, buy finished imports, install automated lines, or replace custom cut stock with engineered alternatives.
o.rationale: Resawing, planing, grading, and sizing remain physical transformations of nonuniform lumber. AI and robotics can automate monitoring and handling, but customers still require an accountable operator to own quality, grade, yield, safety, equipment uptime, and delivery. Vertically integrated customers, imports of finished stock, standardized products, or fully automated captive lines can remove some outsourced quantity.

caveats: The AP evidence concerns construction-material pass-through, not retention of AI savings by planing firms.
No industry study directly measures five-year productivity-benefit retention.
Capture differs between toll conversion and differentiated cut-stock programs.
q.change_evidence: Eligible-firm contracts and invoice histories showing processing-rate resets, lumber-index clauses, open-book provisions, yield ownership, scrap credits, bid frequency, customer concentration, and margin changes after optimizer or scanner deployments.
q.rationale: Standard lumber processing is price transparent and customers can compare conversion spreads, so renewals and competition should transfer a large share of visible labor savings. Operators can retain more from better grade recovery, reduced scrap, fewer line stoppages, faster turnaround, and consistent quality because those gains are less observable and improve service reliability. Toll arrangements may be especially subject to per-unit repricing.

caveats: Patent and research evidence show feasibility, not broad commercial adoption.
Census AI data cover all US businesses rather than planing mills.
The 2024 recordable injury rate of 5.4 per 100 workers raises the burden of safe integration.
rho.change_evidence: Production deployments reporting scanner uptime, grading agreement with certified humans, recovery and yield, false alarms, line stoppages, labor hours per thousand board feet, safety incidents, and sustained staffing changes for at least 12 months.
rho.rationale: Vision-based grading, dimensional measurement, flow monitoring, predictive maintenance, scheduling, and document automation are technically plausible within five years. Implementation is constrained by dusty environments, variable species and moisture, lighting, line speed, legacy controls, safety, scarce integration staff, and the need for human-certified grading or exception handling. LMM plants will capture only part of the exposed opportunity.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sales or ownership transfers for US small-business owners across industries, not completed qualifying control transfers of eligible cut-stock, resawing, and planing firms. Mature industrial ownership may raise propensity above the all-owner 14%, while failed plans and nonqualifying gifts or internal transfers reduce realized qualifying events.
caveats: Plans may not close.
The survey population is broader than industrial LMM firms.
Its ownership-transfer category contains events excluded by the screen.
s5.change_evidence: Owner-age and succession-plan outreach for eligible firms plus five years of closed transactions from PitchBook, Capital IQ, GF Data, Axial, equipment-lender records, brokers, and state filings, distinguishing external control sales from gifts and internal reorganizations.
s5.rationale: Gallup found 14% of working owners planned a sale, IPO, or ownership transfer within five years and 17% among owners age 55 or older. A 16% base reflects likely mature ownership in fragmented wood-processing firms, with a broad range because actual closed control transactions are not observed.

principal_driver: AI-assisted grading, recovery, line monitoring, and scheduling in recurring specification-driven processing relationships.
principal_weakness: Low compensation intensity and transparent conversion pricing constrain the economic value that can be created and retained.

run_meta.title: Cut Stock, Resawing Lumber, and Planing

sources.supports: Defines the industry around cut stock, resawing purchased lumber, and planing and identifies the relevant processing activities.
sources.title: NAICS Code Description: 321912 Cut Stock, Resawing Lumber, and Planing

sources.supports: Shows other-wood-product manufacturing employment is concentrated in assemblers, woodworking and sawing machine operators, material movers, truck operators, supervisors, carpenters, drivers, sales, and management.
sources.title: Data tables for OEWS industry charts, May 2024

sources.supports: Documents loading, unloading, machine operation, inspection, monitoring, packing, recording, and material movement in production support.
sources.title: O*NET: Helpers--Production Workers

sources.supports: Demonstrates AI visual grading for dimensions, knots, cracks, holes, and wane, reporting below-2.5% calibration error and 99-100% defect-detection precision on a lab dataset.
sources.title: Scalable AI-driven automation for visual lumber grading

sources.supports: Describes AI and video monitoring of board integrity, grading, positioning, mechanical failures, and planer- or saw-line flow anomalies with human or robotic intervention.
sources.title: Computer-implemented method and system for managing lumber production line flow using deep learning AI, vision, 3D and robotics

sources.supports: Reports a low late-2023 starting point of 3.8% current US business AI use and 6.5% planned use within six months.
sources.title: Only 3.8% of Businesses Use AI to Produce Goods and Services

sources.supports: Reports 5.4 total recordable injury and illness cases per 100 full-time workers for NAICS 321912 in 2024.
sources.title: BLS Table 1: Incidence rates of nonfatal occupational injuries and illnesses by industry, 2024

sources.supports: Reports 14% of working small-business owners planned a sale, IPO, or transfer within five years, rising to 17% among owners age 55 or older.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Documents construction-material cost pass-through, lumber-price volatility, and the countervailing risk that higher prices reduce housing demand.
sources.title: Tariffs on lumber and appliances set stage for higher costs on new homes and remodeling projects

sources.supports: Projects 1.68 million average annual housing starts in 2025-2029 and 1.52 million in 2030-2033, with substantial financial and demographic uncertainty.
sources.title: The Outlook for Housing Starts

sources.supports: Estimates housing-start elasticity of 0.59, income elasticity of 0.14, and price elasticity of -0.21 for US softwood-lumber demand using 2000-2024 data.
sources.title: Revisiting U.S. softwood lumber demand projections: The central role of residential construction in forest sector outlooks

sources.supports: Reports repair and remodeling as the first- or second-largest market for several wood products and 13.5 billion board feet of softwood lumber used in 2014.
sources.title: Wood products used in residential repair and remodeling in the United States, 2014

title: Cut Stock, Resawing Lumber, and Planing
